title: CSDDD Art. 5 — Risk-based due diligence
text: Member States shall ensure that companies conduct risk-based human rights and environmental due diligence covering own operations, those of subsidiaries, and business partners in the chain of activities. Steps: integrate DD into policies, identify and assess actual and potential adverse impacts, prevent or mitigate, end actual adverse impacts, monitor effectiveness, communicate, provide remediation.

title: CSDDD Art. 7 — Integrating due diligence into company policy
text: Companies shall integrate risk-based DD into all relevant company policies, having a risk management system that covers identification, prevention, mitigation, ending, and remediation of adverse impacts. The DD policy shall be reviewed at least once every 24 months, updated when significant changes occur.

title: CSDDD Art. 10 — Bringing actual adverse impacts to an end
text: Where a company causes or jointly causes an actual adverse impact, it shall take appropriate measures to bring the impact to an end, or where this is not immediately possible, to minimise its extent. Measures shall include: paying damages to affected persons, financial compensation to communities affected, development of a corrective action plan with timelines, milestones, and indicators.

title: CSDDD Art. 11 — Notification and complaints procedure
text: Companies shall establish a fair, publicly available, and accessible procedure for submitting complaints. Categories of complainants: (a) persons affected or with reasonable grounds to believe they may be affected, (b) trade unions and other workers' representatives, (c) civil society organisations active in areas related to the chain of activities.

title: CSDDD Art. 13 — Climate transition plan
text: Companies shall adopt and put into effect a transition plan for climate change mitigation aiming to ensure, through best efforts, that the business model and strategy are compatible with the transition to a sustainable economy and with limiting global warming to 1.5°C in line with the Paris Agreement. The plan shall include time-bound targets for 2030 and in 5-year steps up to 2050.

title: CSDDD Art. 14 — Monitoring
text: Companies shall carry out periodic assessments of their own operations and those of their subsidiaries and business partners to monitor implementation and effectiveness of identification, prevention, mitigation, ending, and minimisation of adverse impacts. Assessments shall be based on qualitative and quantitative indicators and carried out at least once every 12 months.

title: CSDDD Art. 17 — Substantiated concerns
text: Member States shall ensure that any natural or legal person that has substantiated concerns regarding actual or potential adverse impacts has the right to submit those concerns to the supervisory authority. Authorities shall investigate within reasonable time.

title: CSDDD Art. 18 — Mediation
text: Member States shall ensure access to mediation procedures for affected persons. Mediation does not preclude civil liability under Art. 27 nor does it require waiver of judicial remedies.

title: CSDDD Art. 22 — Supervisory authorities
text: Each Member State shall designate one or more supervisory authorities with powers to: order companies to cease infringements, impose pecuniary penalties, require provision of information, and conduct investigations including on-site inspections.

title: CSDDD Art. 26 — Board oversight
text: Directors of in-scope companies shall, when fulfilling their duty to act in the best interest of the company, take into account the consequences of their decisions for sustainability matters, including, where applicable, human rights, climate change, and environmental consequences, in the short, medium, and long term.

title: CSDDD Art. 27 — Civil liability
text: Member States shall ensure that companies are liable for damage caused to a natural or legal person, provided that: (a) the company has intentionally or negligently failed to comply with obligations laid down in Articles 10 and 11 when an adverse impact should have been identified and addressed, and (b) as a result of such failure, damage to the natural or legal person's legal interest protected under national law was caused.
